From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Three-letter air-travel designation for airports and cities

For a list of airports by IATA code, see [Lists of airports § By IATA code](/wiki/Lists_of_airports#By_IATA_code "Lists of airports").

Not to be confused with [ICAO airport code](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/URAluggage.jpg/250px-URAluggage.jpg)](/wiki/File:URAluggage.jpg)

A baggage tag for a flight heading to [Oral Ak Zhol Airport](/wiki/Oral_Ak_Zhol_Airport "Oral Ak Zhol Airport"), whose IATA airport code is "URA"

An **IATA airport code**, also known as an **IATA location identifier**, **IATA station code**, or simply a **[location identifier](/wiki/Location_identifier "Location identifier")**, is a unique three-letter [geocode](/wiki/Geocode "Geocode") designating many [airports](/wiki/Airport "Airport"), cities (with one or more airports) and [metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") (cities with more than one airport) around the world, defined by the [International Air Transport Association](/wiki/International_Air_Transport_Association "International Air Transport Association") (IATA).[[1]](#cite_note-1) The characters prominently displayed on [baggage tags](/wiki/Bag_tag "Bag tag") attached at [airport check-in](/wiki/Airport_check-in "Airport check-in") desks are an example of a way these codes are used.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The assignment of these codes is governed by IATA Resolution 763,[[5]](#cite_note-5) and it is administered by the IATA's headquarters in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), Canada. The codes are published semi-annually in the IATA Airline Coding Directory.

IATA also provides codes for airport handling entities and for certain railway stations, bus stations and ferry ports.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[Alphabetical lists of airports](/wiki/Lists_of_airports_by_IATA_and_ICAO_code "Lists of airports by IATA and ICAO code") sorted by IATA code are available. A [list of railway station codes](/wiki/List_of_IATA-indexed_railway_stations "List of IATA-indexed railway stations"), shared in agreements between airlines and rail lines such as [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), [SNCF](/wiki/SNCF "SNCF"), and [Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn"), is available. However, many railway administrations have their own list of codes for their stations, such as [Amtrak station codes](/wiki/List_of_Amtrak_stations "List of Amtrak stations").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Airport codes arose out of the convenience that the practice brought pilots for location identification in the 1930s. Initially, pilots in the United States used the two-letter code from the [National Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service") (NWS) for identifying cities. This system became unmanageable for cities and towns without an NWS identifier, and the use of two letters allowed only a few hundred combinations; a three-letter system of airport codes was implemented. This system allowed for 17,576 permutations, assuming all letters can be used in conjunction with each other.[[7]](#cite_note-sky-7)

In the early days of aviation, airport codes frequently adopted (or were required to comply with) the naming conventions previously established by [weather stations](/wiki/Weather_stations "Weather stations"), [railway stations](/wiki/List_of_IATA-indexed_railway_stations,_bus_stations_and_ferry_terminals "List of IATA-indexed railway stations, bus stations and ferry terminals"), and other commercial or governmental outposts that communicated by radio or telegraph. The code names for those stations, which usually predated the use of [two-way radio](/wiki/Two-way_radio "Two-way radio") and even [radiotelephony](/wiki/Radiotelephony "Radiotelephony") itself, reflected the need for terse, standardized signaling patterns that could be easily transmitted and correctly received via [radiotelegraphy](/wiki/Radiotelegraphy "Radiotelegraphy") and [Morse code](/wiki/Morse_code "Morse code").

## Naming conventions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Naming conventions")]

### National policies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: National policies")]

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Canada")]

Canada's unusual codes, which bear little to no similarity with any conventional abbreviation to the city's name (such as [YUL](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al%E2%80%93Trudeau_International_Airport "Montréal–Trudeau International Airport") in [Montréal](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al "Montréal") and [YYZ](/wiki/Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport "Toronto Pearson International Airport") in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")), originated from the two-letter codes used to identify weather reporting stations in the 1930s. The letters preceding the two-letter code follow the following format:

* Y – for *"Yes"*, this letter was used when the station shared its location with an airport.
* W – for *"Without"*, when the weather-reporting station did not share its location with an airport.
* U – used when the station was located together with a [non-directional beacon](/wiki/Non-directional_beacon "Non-directional beacon") (NDB).
* X – suggesting that the last two letters of a code were already in use by a Canadian airport.
* Z – indicated that an airport code had been used for the identification of an airport in the U.S.

Most large airports in Canada have codes that begin with the letter "Y",[[8]](#cite_note-8)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] although not all "Y" codes are Canadian (for example, [YUM](/wiki/Yuma_International_Airport "Yuma International Airport") for [Yuma, Arizona](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona"), and [YNT](/wiki/Yantai_Penglai_International_Airport "Yantai Penglai International Airport") for [Yantai](/wiki/Yantai "Yantai"), China), and not all Canadian airports start with the letter "Y" (for example, [ZBF](/wiki/Bathurst_Airport_(New_Brunswick) "Bathurst Airport (New Brunswick)") for [Bathurst, New Brunswick](/wiki/Bathurst,_New_Brunswick "Bathurst, New Brunswick")). Many Canadian airports have a code that starts with W, X or Z, but none of these are major airports. When the [Canadian transcontinental railways](/wiki/Transcontinental_railroad#Canada "Transcontinental railroad") were built, each station was assigned its own two-letter [Morse code](/wiki/Morse_code "Morse code"):

* VR for [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver")
* TZ for [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")
* QB for [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City")
* WG for [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg")
* SJ for [Saint John](/wiki/Saint_John,_New_Brunswick "Saint John, New Brunswick")
* YC for [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary")
* OW for [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa")
* EG for [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton")

When the Canadian government established airports, it used the existing railway codes for them as well. If the airport had a weather station, authorities added a "Y" to the front of the code, meaning "Yes" to indicate it had a weather station or some other letter to indicate it did not. When international codes were created in cooperation with the United States, because "Y" was seldom used in the United States, Canada simply used the weather station codes for its airports, changing the "Y" to a "Z" if it conflicted with an airport code already in use. The result is that most major Canadian airport codes start with "Y" followed by two letters in the city's name, based on the transcontiential railroad two-letter Morse code, as mentioned above (for example, [YOW](/wiki/Ottawa_Macdonald%E2%80%93Cartier_International_Airport "Ottawa Macdonald–Cartier International Airport") for [**O**tta**w**a](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), [YWG](/wiki/Winnipeg_James_Armstrong_Richardson_International_Airport "Winnipeg James Armstrong Richardson International Airport") for [**W**innipe**g**](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg"), [YYC](/wiki/Calgary_International_Airport "Calgary International Airport") for [**C**algar**y**](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), or [YVR](/wiki/Vancouver_International_Airport "Vancouver International Airport") for [**V**ancouve**r**](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver")), whereas other Canadian airports append the two-letter code of the radio beacons that were the closest to the actual airport, such as [YQX](/wiki/Gander_International_Airport "Gander International Airport") in [Gander](/wiki/Gander,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Gander, Newfoundland and Labrador") or [YXS](/wiki/Prince_George_Airport "Prince George Airport") in [Prince George](/wiki/Prince_George,_British_Columbia "Prince George, British Columbia").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Four of the ten provincial capital airports in Canada have ended up with codes beginning with YY, including:

* [YYZ](/wiki/Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport "Toronto Pearson International Airport") for [Toronto, Ontario](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")
* [YYJ](/wiki/Victoria_International_Airport "Victoria International Airport") for [Victoria, British Columbia](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia")
* [YYT](/wiki/St._John%27s_International_Airport "St. John's International Airport") for [St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/St._John%27s,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador")
* [YYG](/wiki/Charlottetown_Airport "Charlottetown Airport") for [Charlottetown, Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Charlottetown "Charlottetown")

Canada's largest airport is YYZ[[9]](#cite_note-9) for [Toronto Pearson](/wiki/Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport "Toronto Pearson International Airport") (as YTZ was already allocated to [Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport](/wiki/Billy_Bishop_Toronto_City_Airport "Billy Bishop Toronto City Airport"), the airport was given the station code of [Malton, Mississauga](/wiki/Malton,_Mississauga "Malton, Mississauga"), where it is located). YUL is used for [Montréal–Trudeau](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al%E2%80%93Trudeau_International_Airport "Montréal–Trudeau International Airport") (UL was the ID code for the beacon in the city of [Kirkland](/wiki/Kirkland,_Quebec "Kirkland, Quebec"). While these codes make it difficult for the public to associate them with a particular Canadian city, some codes have become popular in usage despite their cryptic nature, particularly at the largest airports. Toronto's code has entered pop culture in the form of "[YYZ](/wiki/YYZ_(song) "YYZ (song)")", a song by the rock band [Rush](/wiki/Rush_(band) "Rush (band)"), which utilizes the Morse code signal as a musical motif. Some airports have started using their IATA codes as [brand names](/wiki/Brand_name "Brand name"), such as [Calgary International Airport](/wiki/Calgary_International_Airport "Calgary International Airport") (YYC)[[10]](#cite_note-10) and [Vancouver International Airport](/wiki/Vancouver_International_Airport "Vancouver International Airport") (YVR).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

#### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Numerous New Zealand airports use codes that contain the letter Z, to distinguish them from similar airport names in other countries. Some examples are [HLZ](/wiki/Hamilton_Airport_(New_Zealand) "Hamilton Airport (New Zealand)") for [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_New_Zealand "Hamilton, New Zealand"), [ZQN](/wiki/Queenstown_Airport "Queenstown Airport") for [Queenstown](/wiki/Queenstown,_New_Zealand "Queenstown, New Zealand"), and [WSZ](/wiki/Westport_Airport_(New_Zealand) "Westport Airport (New Zealand)") for [Westport](/wiki/Westport,_New_Zealand "Westport, New Zealand").

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: United States")]

Since the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") reserved "N" codes, and to prevent confusion with [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") [broadcast call signs](/wiki/Call_signs_in_North_America "Call signs in North America"), which begin with "W" or "K", the airports of certain U.S. cities whose name begins with one of these letters had to adopt "irregular" airport codes:

* [APC](/wiki/Napa_County_Airport "Napa County Airport") for [Napa, California](/wiki/Napa,_California "Napa, California")[[7]](#cite_note-sky-7)
* [ASH](/wiki/Nashua_Airport "Nashua Airport") for [Nashua, New Hampshire](/wiki/Nashua,_New_Hampshire "Nashua, New Hampshire") (at Boire Field)
* [BNA](/wiki/Nashville_International_Airport "Nashville International Airport") for [Nashville, Tennessee](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee") (whose airport's original name was *Berry Field*)
* [EWR](/wiki/Newark_Liberty_International_Airport "Newark Liberty International Airport") for [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey")
* [EYW](/wiki/Key_West_International_Airport "Key West International Airport") for [Key West, Florida](/wiki/Key_West "Key West")
* [HVN](/wiki/Tweed_New_Haven_Airport "Tweed New Haven Airport") for [New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut")
* [ILM](/wiki/Wilmington_International_Airport "Wilmington International Airport") for [Wilmington, North Carolina](/wiki/Wilmington,_North_Carolina "Wilmington, North Carolina")
* [MCI](/wiki/Kansas_City_International_Airport "Kansas City International Airport") for [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri") (whose airport's original name was *Mid-Continent International Airport*)
* [MSY](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong_New_Orleans_International_Airport "Louis Armstrong New Orleans International Airport") for [New Orleans, Louisiana](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") (named for the Moisant Stock Yards previously on the airport's site)
* [OME](/wiki/Nome_Airport "Nome Airport") for [Nome, Alaska](/wiki/Nome,_Alaska "Nome, Alaska")
* [ORF](/wiki/Norfolk_International_Airport "Norfolk International Airport") for [Norfolk, Virginia](/wiki/Norfolk,_Virginia "Norfolk, Virginia")

This practice is not followed outside the United States:

* [Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur") is [KUL](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_International_Airport "Kuala Lumpur International Airport")
* [Naples, Italy](/wiki/Naples "Naples") is [NAP](/wiki/Naples_International_Airport "Naples International Airport")
* [Nassau, The Bahamas](/wiki/Nassau,_The_Bahamas "Nassau, The Bahamas") is [NAS](/wiki/Lynden_Pindling_International_Airport "Lynden Pindling International Airport")
* [Warsaw, Poland](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") is [WAW](/wiki/Warsaw_Chopin_Airport "Warsaw Chopin Airport")

In addition, since [three letter codes starting with Q](/wiki/Q_code "Q code") are widely used in radio communication, cities whose name begins with "Q" also had to find alternate codes, as in the case of:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Qiqihar](/wiki/Qiqihar "Qiqihar") ([NDG](/wiki/Qiqihar_Sanjiazi_Airport "Qiqihar Sanjiazi Airport"))
* [Quetta](/wiki/Quetta "Quetta") ([UET](/wiki/Quetta_International_Airport "Quetta International Airport"))
* [Quito](/wiki/Quito "Quito") ([UIO](/wiki/Mariscal_Sucre_International_Airport "Mariscal Sucre International Airport"))
* [Quimper](/wiki/Quimper "Quimper") ([UIP](/wiki/Quimper%E2%80%93Cornouaille_Airport "Quimper–Cornouaille Airport"))

Even this practice is not followed universally. For instance [Owerri](/wiki/Owerri "Owerri") in Nigeria uses the code [QOW](/wiki/Sam_Mbakwe_Airport "Sam Mbakwe Airport").

IATA codes should not be confused with the [FAA identifiers](/wiki/Location_identifier#FAA_identifier "Location identifier") of U.S. airports. Most FAA identifiers agree with the corresponding IATA codes, but some do not, such as [Saipan](/wiki/Saipan_International_Airport "Saipan International Airport"), whose FAA identifier is GSN and its IATA code is SPN, and some coincide with IATA codes of non-U.S. airports.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Naming conventions in general

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Naming conventions in general")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **contains an excessive amount of intricate [detail](/wiki/Wikipedia:DETAIL "Wikipedia:DETAIL")**. Please help [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit) by [spinning off](/wiki/Wikipedia:Content_forks#Article_spinoffs:_.22Summary_style.22_meta-articles_and_summary_sections "Wikipedia:Content forks") or [relocating](/wiki/Wikipedia:Handling_trivia#Recommendations_for_handling_trivia "Wikipedia:Handling trivia") relevant information and removing excessive detail that goes against [Wikipedia's inclusion policy](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not"). *(January 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Many airport codes consist of the first three letters of the city where the airport is located, for instance:

* [AMS](/wiki/Amsterdam_Airport_Schiphol "Amsterdam Airport Schiphol") for [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam")
* [DEL](/wiki/Indira_Gandhi_International_Airport "Indira Gandhi International Airport") for [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi")
* [SIN](/wiki/Changi_Airport "Changi Airport") for [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")

The code may also be another combination of the letters in the city's name, such as:

* [GDL](/wiki/Miguel_Hidalgo_y_Costilla_International_Airport "Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla International Airport") for [Guadalajara](/wiki/Guadalajara "Guadalajara")
* [HKG](/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Airport "Hong Kong International Airport") for [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong")
* [WAW](/wiki/Warsaw_Chopin_Airport "Warsaw Chopin Airport") for [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw")

Sometimes the airport code reflects pronunciation, rather than spelling, for example:

* [NAN](/wiki/Nadi_International_Airport "Nadi International Airport"), which reflects the pronunciation of "Nadi" as [[ˈnandi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Fijian "Help:IPA/Fijian") in [Fijian](/wiki/Fijian_language "Fijian language"), where "d" is realized as the [prenasalized stop](/wiki/Prenasalized_consonant "Prenasalized consonant") [ⁿd]

For many reasons, some airport codes do not fit the normal scheme described above. Some airports, for example, cross several municipalities or regions, and therefore use codes derived from a combination of their letters, resulting in:

* DFW for [Dallas/Fort Worth](/wiki/Dallas/Fort_Worth_International_Airport "Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport")
* DTW for [Detroit–Wayne County](/wiki/Detroit_Metropolitan_Wayne_County_Airport "Detroit Metropolitan Wayne County Airport")
* LBA for [Leeds–Bradford (Airport)](/wiki/Leeds_Bradford_International_Airport "Leeds Bradford International Airport")
* MSP for [Minneapolis–Saint Paul](/wiki/Minneapolis%E2%80%93Saint_Paul_International_Airport "Minneapolis–Saint Paul International Airport")
* RDU for [Raleigh–Durham](/wiki/Raleigh%E2%80%93Durham_International_Airport "Raleigh–Durham International Airport")

Other airports—particularly those serving cities with multiple airports—have codes derived from the name of the airport itself, for instance:

* JFK for New York's [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport")
* LHR for London's [Heathrow Airport](/wiki/Heathrow_Airport "Heathrow Airport")
* CDG for Paris's [Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle_International_Airport "Charles de Gaulle International Airport") (see below)

This is also true with some cities with a single airport (even if there is more than one airport in the metropolitan area of said city), such as BDL for [Hartford, Connecticut](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut")'s [Bradley International Airport](/wiki/Bradley_International_Airport "Bradley International Airport") or [Baltimore's](/wiki/Baltimore,_Maryland "Baltimore, Maryland") BWI, for [Baltimore/Washington International Airport](/wiki/Baltimore/Washington_International_Airport "Baltimore/Washington International Airport"); however, the latter also serves [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), alongside [Dulles International Airport](/wiki/Dulles_International_Airport "Dulles International Airport") (IAD, for International Airport Dulles) and [Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_Washington_National_Airport "Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport") (DCA, for District of Columbia Airport).[[7]](#cite_note-sky-7)

The code also sometimes comes from the airport's former name, such as [Orlando International Airport](/wiki/Orlando_International_Airport "Orlando International Airport")'s MCO (for McCoy Air Force Base), or Chicago's [O'Hare International Airport](/wiki/O%27Hare_International_Airport "O'Hare International Airport"), which is coded ORD for its original name: Orchard Field. In rare cases, the code comes from the airport's unofficial name, such as [Kahului Airport](/wiki/Kahului_Airport "Kahului Airport")'s OGG (for local aviation pioneer Jimmy Hogg).

#### Cities with multiple commercial airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cities with multiple commercial airports")]

When different cities with the same name each have an airport, they need to be assigned different codes. Examples include:

* [Juan Santamaría International Airport](/wiki/Juan_Santamar%C3%ADa_International_Airport "Juan Santamaría International Airport") (SJO) is in [Alajuela](/wiki/Alajuela_Province "Alajuela Province"), serving the capital [San José de Costa Rica](/wiki/San_Jos%C3%A9,_Costa_Rica "San José, Costa Rica"). While [Norman Y. Mineta San Jose International Airport](/wiki/Norman_Y._Mineta_San_Jose_International_Airport "Norman Y. Mineta San Jose International Airport") (SJC) is in [San Jose, California](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California"), the United States.
* [Birmingham–Shuttlesworth International Airport](/wiki/Birmingham%E2%80%93Shuttlesworth_International_Airport "Birmingham–Shuttlesworth International Airport") (BHM) is in [Birmingham, Alabama](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"), the United States and [Birmingham Airport](/wiki/Birmingham_Airport "Birmingham Airport") (BHX) is in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), England, United Kingdom.
* [Portland International Jetport](/wiki/Portland_International_Jetport "Portland International Jetport") (PWM) is in [Portland, Maine](/wiki/Portland,_Maine "Portland, Maine"), while [Portland International Airport](/wiki/Portland_International_Airport "Portland International Airport") (PDX) is in [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon").
* [Manchester Airport](/wiki/Manchester_Airport "Manchester Airport") (MAN) is in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), England, United Kingdom, while [Manchester-Boston Regional Airport](/wiki/Manchester%E2%80%93Boston_Regional_Airport "Manchester–Boston Regional Airport") (MHT) is in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester,_New_Hampshire "Manchester, New Hampshire"), [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), United States.
* [Arturo Merino Benítez International Airport](/wiki/Arturo_Merino_Ben%C3%ADtez_International_Airport "Arturo Merino Benítez International Airport") (SCL) is in [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago"), Chile; while [Antonio Maceo Airport](/wiki/Antonio_Maceo_Airport "Antonio Maceo Airport") (SCU) is in [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago_de_Cuba "Santiago de Cuba"), Cuba; [Santiago–Rosalía de Castro Airport](/wiki/Santiago%E2%80%93Rosal%C3%ADa_de_Castro_Airport "Santiago–Rosalía de Castro Airport") (SCQ) is in [Santiago de Compostela](/wiki/Santiago_de_Compostela "Santiago de Compostela"), Spain; and [Cibao International Airport](/wiki/Cibao_International_Airport "Cibao International Airport") (STI) serves [Santiago de los Caballeros](/wiki/Santiago_de_los_Caballeros "Santiago de los Caballeros"), Dominican Republic.
* [Ingeniero Ambrosio L.V. Taravella International Airport](/wiki/Ingeniero_Ambrosio_L.V._Taravella_International_Airport "Ingeniero Ambrosio L.V. Taravella International Airport") (COR) is in [Córdoba, Argentina](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Argentina "Córdoba, Argentina"); while [Córdoba Airport](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba_Airport "Córdoba Airport") (ODB) is in [Córdoba, Spain](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Spain "Córdoba, Spain").

Sometimes, a new airport is built, replacing the old one, leaving the city's new "major" airport (or the only remaining airport) code to no longer correspond with the city's name. The original airport in Nashville, Tennessee, was built in 1936 as part of the Works Progress Administration and called Berry Field with the designation, BNA. A new facility known as Nashville International Airport was built in 1987 but still uses BNA. This is in conjunction to rules aimed to avoid confusion that seem to apply in the United States, which state that "the first and second letters or second and third letters of an identifier may not be duplicated with less than 200 [nautical miles](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile") separation."[[7]](#cite_note-sky-7) Thus, Washington, D.C. area's three airports all have radically different codes: IAD for [Washington–Dulles](/wiki/Washington_Dulles_International_Airport "Washington Dulles International Airport"), DCA for [Washington–Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_Washington_National_Airport "Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport") (District of Columbia Airport), and BWI for [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore_Airport "Baltimore Airport") (Baltimore–Washington International, formerly BAL).[[7]](#cite_note-sky-7) Since HOU is used for [William P. Hobby Airport](/wiki/William_P._Hobby_Airport "William P. Hobby Airport"), the new [Houston–Intercontinental](/wiki/Houston%E2%80%93Intercontinental "Houston–Intercontinental") became IAH.[[7]](#cite_note-sky-7) The code BKK was originally assigned to [Bangkok–Don Mueang](/wiki/Don_Mueang_International_Airport "Don Mueang International Airport") and was later transferred to [Suvarnabhumi Airport](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi_Airport "Suvarnabhumi Airport"), while the former adopted DMK. Meanwhile, [Tan Son Nhat Airport](/wiki/Tan_Son_Nhat_International_Airport "Tan Son Nhat International Airport") keeps its [Saigon](/wiki/Saigon "Saigon")-inspired SGN code even though the city has long been renamed to [Ho Chi Minh City](/wiki/Ho_Chi_Minh_City "Ho Chi Minh City"), and the city's new primary airport [Long Thanh International](/wiki/Long_Thanh_International_Airport "Long Thanh International Airport") adopts a brand new code LTH after its own name rather than any derivatives from the name of the city that it serves or the legacy SGN designation. The code ISK was originally assigned to [Gandhinagar Airport](/wiki/Gandhinagar_Airport "Gandhinagar Airport") (Nashik's old airport) and later on transferred to [Ozar Airport](/wiki/Nashik_Airport "Nashik Airport") (Nashik's current airport). [Shanghai–Hongqiao](/wiki/Shanghai_Hongqiao_International_Airport "Shanghai Hongqiao International Airport") retained the code SHA, while the newer [Shanghai–Pudong](/wiki/Shanghai_Pudong_International_Airport "Shanghai Pudong International Airport") adopted PVG. The opposite was true for [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"): the airport [Berlin–Tegel](/wiki/Berlin_Tegel_Airport "Berlin Tegel Airport") used the code TXL, while its smaller counterpart [Berlin–Schönefeld](/wiki/Berlin_Sch%C3%B6nefeld_Airport "Berlin Schönefeld Airport") used SXF; the [Berlin Brandenburg Airport](/wiki/Berlin_Brandenburg_Airport "Berlin Brandenburg Airport"), which reuses much of Schönefeld's infrastructure, has the airport code BER, which is also part of its branding. The airports of [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg_Airport "Hamburg Airport") (HAM) and [Hannover](/wiki/Hannover_Airport "Hannover Airport") (HAJ) are less than 100 nautical miles (190 km) apart and therefore share the same first and middle letters, indicating that this rule might be followed only in Germany.

Some airport codes are based on previous names associated with a present airport, often with a military heritage. These include:

* Chicago's [O'Hare](/wiki/O%27Hare_International_Airport "O'Hare International Airport"), which is assigned ORD based on its old name of Orchard Field. It was expanded and renamed O'Hare in the mid-1950s.
* [Rickenbacker International Airport](/wiki/Rickenbacker_International_Airport "Rickenbacker International Airport") uses LCK, for its former name of Lockbourne Air Force Base.
* [Travis Air Force Base](/wiki/Travis_Air_Force_Base "Travis Air Force Base") uses SUU, for its former name of Fairfield-Suisun Army Air Base.
* [North Texas Regional Airport](/wiki/North_Texas_Regional_Airport "North Texas Regional Airport") uses PNX, for its former name of [Perrin Air Force Station](/wiki/Perrin_Air_Force_Station "Perrin Air Force Station").
* [Fresno Yosemite International Airport](/wiki/Fresno_Yosemite_International_Airport "Fresno Yosemite International Airport") uses the code FAT, derived from a previous name of the airport, Fresno Air Terminal.
* [Orlando International Airport](/wiki/Orlando_International_Airport "Orlando International Airport") was founded as Orlando Army Air Field #2 but uses MCO for having been renamed [McCoy Air Force Base](/wiki/McCoy_Air_Force_Base "McCoy Air Force Base") in 1959 in honor of a wing commander who crashed at the field in 1958. It was converted in the early 1960s to joint civilian/military use and renamed Orlando Jetport at McCoy, then renamed Orlando International Airport in the early 1980s.
* [Kansas City International Airport](/wiki/Kansas_City_International_Airport "Kansas City International Airport") uses MCI, for its original name of Mid-Continent International Airport. The airport is referred to locally as "KCI", and attempts have been made to change its IATA code to match; however, "KCI" is already used for an airport in [Timor-Leste](/wiki/Timor-Leste "Timor-Leste").
* [Spokane International Airport](/wiki/Spokane_International_Airport "Spokane International Airport") was so named in 1960 but goes by GEG because it was built on the former Geiger Field, renamed in 1941 for Major [Harold Geiger](/wiki/Harold_Geiger "Harold Geiger") when the US Army acquired it.
* [Louis Armstrong New Orleans International Airport](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong_New_Orleans_International_Airport "Louis Armstrong New Orleans International Airport") was originally named Moisant Field after daredevil aviator [John Moisant](/wiki/John_Moisant "John Moisant"), who died in 1910 in an airplane crash on agricultural land where the airport is now located. Its IATA code MSY was derived from Moisant Stock Yards, as Lakefront Airport retained the code NEW.
* [Lehigh Valley International Airport](/wiki/Lehigh_Valley_International_Airport "Lehigh Valley International Airport") uses ABE, for its former name of [Allentown](/wiki/Allentown,_Pennsylvania "Allentown, Pennsylvania")–[Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem,_Pennsylvania "Bethlehem, Pennsylvania")–[Easton](/wiki/Easton,_Pennsylvania "Easton, Pennsylvania") International Airport.
* [William R. Fairchild International Airport](/wiki/William_R._Fairchild_International_Airport "William R. Fairchild International Airport") uses CLM, for its former name of Clallam County Municipal Landing Field.
* [Chicago Executive Airport](/wiki/Chicago_Executive_Airport "Chicago Executive Airport") uses PWK, for its former name, Palwaukee Municipal Airport (which was derived from its location on Palatine Road and [Milwaukee Avenue](/wiki/Milwaukee_Avenue,_Chicago "Milwaukee Avenue, Chicago")).
* [Dallas Executive Airport](/wiki/Dallas_Executive_Airport "Dallas Executive Airport") used RBD, for its former name, Redbird Airport.
* [TSTC Waco Airport](/wiki/TSTC_Waco_Airport "TSTC Waco Airport") uses CNW, as it was formerly [Connally Air Force Base](/wiki/James_Connally_Air_Force_Base "James Connally Air Force Base").
* [Glacier Park International Airport](/wiki/Glacier_Park_International_Airport "Glacier Park International Airport") uses FCA, for its former name Flathead County Airport.

Some airports are named for an administrative division or nearby city, rather than the one they are located in:

* [Juan Santamaría International Airport](/wiki/Juan_Santamar%C3%ADa_International_Airport "Juan Santamaría International Airport") is located in [Alajuela province](/wiki/Alajuela_Province "Alajuela Province"), but since it is so close to the capital city of [San José, Costa Rica](/wiki/San_Jos%C3%A9,_Costa_Rica "San José, Costa Rica"), the airport serves the whole [Central Valley](/wiki/Costa_Rican_Central_Valley "Costa Rican Central Valley") using SJO.
* [Grand Strand Airport](/wiki/Grand_Strand_Airport "Grand Strand Airport") uses CRE for the former municipality of [Crescent Beach, South Carolina](/wiki/Crescent_Beach,_South_Carolina "Crescent Beach, South Carolina").
* [Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky International Airport](/wiki/Cincinnati/Northern_Kentucky_International_Airport "Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky International Airport") is located in an unincorporated area of [Boone County, Kentucky](/wiki/Boone_County,_Kentucky "Boone County, Kentucky") and uses CVG after the nearby city of [Covington](/wiki/Covington,_Kentucky "Covington, Kentucky") (located in neighboring [Kenton County](/wiki/Kenton_County,_Kentucky "Kenton County, Kentucky")).
* [San Ignacio Town Airstrip](/wiki/San_Ignacio_Town_Airstrip "San Ignacio Town Airstrip"), located in [San Ignacio, Belize](/wiki/San_Ignacio,_Belize "San Ignacio, Belize"), uses CYD because it is located in the [Cayo District](/wiki/Cayo_District "Cayo District").
* [Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_Washington_National_Airport "Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport") in [Crystal City, Virginia](/wiki/Crystal_City,_Virginia "Crystal City, Virginia") uses DCA for the [District of Columbia](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") (DC) and Arlington.
* [Prince Naif bin Abdulaziz International Airport](/wiki/Prince_Naif_bin_Abdulaziz_International_Airport "Prince Naif bin Abdulaziz International Airport") in [Buraidah](/wiki/Buraidah "Buraidah"), Saudi Arabia uses ELQ for the [Al-Qassim Province](/wiki/Al-Qassim_Province "Al-Qassim Province") (El Qassim)
* [Damazin Airport](/wiki/Damazin_Airport "Damazin Airport") in Sudan uses RSS, for the nearby [Roseires Dam](/wiki/Roseires_Dam "Roseires Dam").

Other airport codes are of obscure origin, and each has its own peculiarities:

* Nashville uses [BNA](/wiki/Nashville_Airport "Nashville Airport") for its former name as Berry Field, henceforth Berry Nashville Airport.
* [Louisville](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky") Muhammad Ali International Airport is [SDF](/wiki/Louisville_International_Airport "Louisville International Airport") for Standiford Field, its original name ([Elisha David Standiford](/wiki/Elisha_David_Standiford "Elisha David Standiford") who, as a businessman and legislator, played an important role in Louisville transportation history and owned part of the land on which the airport was built.)[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* [Knoxville](/wiki/Knoxville "Knoxville") uses [TYS](/wiki/McGhee_Tyson_Airport "McGhee Tyson Airport") for [Charles McGhee Tyson](/wiki/Charles_McGhee_Tyson "Charles McGhee Tyson"), whose family donated the land for the first airport in Knoxville
* [Kahului](/wiki/Kahului "Kahului"), the main gateway into [Maui](/wiki/Maui "Maui"), uses [OGG](/wiki/Kahului_Airport "Kahului Airport") in homage to Hawaiian aviation pioneer Bertram J. Hogg
* [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), Australia, uses [OOL](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Airport "Gold Coast Airport") due to its former name as Coolangatta Airport, [named after the suburb](/wiki/Coolangatta "Coolangatta") in which it is located
* [Sunshine Coast](/wiki/Sunshine_Coast,_Queensland "Sunshine Coast, Queensland"), Australia, uses [MCY](/wiki/Sunshine_Coast_Airport "Sunshine Coast Airport") due to its former names Maroochydore Airport and Maroochydore-Sunshine Coast Airport. It is actually located in [Marcoola](/wiki/Marcoola,_Queensland "Marcoola, Queensland") rather than Maroochydore.
* [Buli Airport](/wiki/Buli_Airport "Buli Airport") uses PGQ, for its location in the [Pekaulang](/wiki/Pekaulang "Pekaulang") administrative division.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [New River Valley Airport](/wiki/New_River_Valley_Airport "New River Valley Airport") uses PSK for its location in [Pulaski County, Virginia](/wiki/Pulaski_County,_Virginia "Pulaski County, Virginia").[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Río Amazonas Airport](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_Amazonas_Airport "Río Amazonas Airport") uses PTZ for its location in [Pastaza Province](/wiki/Pastaza_Province "Pastaza Province").[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* [Brackett Field](/wiki/Brackett_Field "Brackett Field") uses POC, as it was named after a flying enthusiast and faculty member of nearby [Pomona College](/wiki/Pomona_College "Pomona College").[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* [Yan'an Nanniwan Airport](/wiki/Yan%27an_Nanniwan_Airport "Yan'an Nanniwan Airport") inherited the ENY code from the city of [Yan'an](/wiki/Yan%27an "Yan'an")'s old airport, [Yan'an Ershilipu Airport](/wiki/Yan%27an_Ershilipu_Airport "Yan'an Ershilipu Airport").
* [Northwest Florida Beaches International Airport](/wiki/Northwest_Florida_Beaches_International_Airport "Northwest Florida Beaches International Airport") uses the code ECP, which when proposed was thought it could stand for "Everyone Can Party"[[17]](#cite_note-Story_of_ECP-17)

In Asia, codes that do not correspond with their city's names include [Niigata](/wiki/Niigata_(city) "Niigata (city)")'s [KIJ](/wiki/Niigata_Airport "Niigata Airport"), [Nanchang](/wiki/Nanchang "Nanchang")'s [KHN](/wiki/Nanchang_Airport "Nanchang Airport") and [Pyongyang](/wiki/Pyongyang "Pyongyang")'s [FNJ](/wiki/Pyongyang_International_Airport "Pyongyang International Airport").

A rare exception to airport codes staying constant even after name changes is the [primary international airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") in New York City. Initially known as Idlewild Airport and given the code IDL, it was changed to John F. Kennedy International Airport in 1963 following the [assassination of the president](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy"). The name change came with a new code, KIA for "**K**ennedy **I**nternational **A**irport". However, this code would gain a negative association with the onset of the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") as the term "[killed in action](/wiki/Killed_in_action "Killed in action")" was commonly abbreviated KIA. Airport authorities applied to the IATA to change the code in 1968, giving the airport its well-known code of JFK.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### Multiple codes for a single airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Multiple codes for a single airport")]

[EuroAirport Basel Mulhouse Freiburg](/wiki/EuroAirport_Basel_Mulhouse_Freiburg "EuroAirport Basel Mulhouse Freiburg"), which serves three countries, has three airport codes: BSL, MLH, EAP.

* The French part of the airport is assigned MLH, for [Mulhouse](/wiki/Mulhouse "Mulhouse"), France
* The Swiss part of the airport is assigned BSL, for [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel"), Switzerland
* The airport also has a Metropolitan Area Code, EAP, for EuroAirport.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

#### Airport codes using the English name of the city

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Airport codes using the English name of the city")]

Some cities have a local name in their respective language which is different from the name in English, yet the airport code represents *only* the English name. Examples include:

* [BKK](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi_Airport "Suvarnabhumi Airport") – [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), Thailand (Thai: กรุงเทพ, romanized: *Krung Thep*)
* [CAI](/wiki/Cairo_International_Airport "Cairo International Airport") – [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), Egypt (Arabic: القاهرة, romanized: *al-Qāhirah*)
* [CGN](/wiki/Cologne_Bonn_Airport "Cologne Bonn Airport") – [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), Germany (German: *Köln*)
* [CPH](/wiki/Copenhagen_Airport "Copenhagen Airport") – [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), Denmark (Danish: *København*)
* [DEL](/wiki/Indira_Gandhi_International_Airport "Indira Gandhi International Airport") – [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), India (Hindi: दिल्ली, romanized: *Dilli*)
* [DUB](/wiki/Dublin_Airport "Dublin Airport") – [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), Ireland (Irish: *Baile Átha Cliath*)
* [FLR](/wiki/Florence_Airport "Florence Airport") – [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), Italy (Italian: *Firenze*)
* [GVA](/wiki/Geneva_Airport "Geneva Airport") – [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva"), Switzerland (French: *Genève*)
* [HAV](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mart%C3%AD_International_Airport "José Martí International Airport") – [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana"), Cuba (Spanish: *La Habana*)
* [LKO](/wiki/Chaudhary_Charan_Singh_International_Airport "Chaudhary Charan Singh International Airport") – [Lucknow](/wiki/Lucknow "Lucknow"), India (Hindi: लखनऊ, romanized: *Lakhnau*)
* [PRG](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Havel_Airport_Prague "Václav Havel Airport Prague") – [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), Czechia (Czech: *Praha*)
* [VCE](/wiki/Venice_Marco_Polo_Airport "Venice Marco Polo Airport") – [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), Italy (Italian: *Venezia*)
* [VIE](/wiki/Vienna_International_Airport "Vienna International Airport") – [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), Austria (German: *Wien*)

#### Scarcity of codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Scarcity of codes")]

Due to scarcity of codes (the three-character code is used by a maximum of 17,576 airports worldwide only), some airports are given codes with letters not found in their names:

* [ACI](/wiki/Alderney_Airport "Alderney Airport") ("Alderney, Channel Islands") for [Alderney](/wiki/Alderney "Alderney"), [Guernsey](/wiki/Bailiwick_of_Guernsey "Bailiwick of Guernsey")
* [BGO](/wiki/Bergen_Airport,_Flesland "Bergen Airport, Flesland") for [Bergen, Norway](/wiki/Bergen,_Norway "Bergen, Norway")
* [CWB](/wiki/Afonso_Pena_International_Airport "Afonso Pena International Airport") for [Curitiba, Brazil](/wiki/Curitiba,_Brazil "Curitiba, Brazil")
* [DAD](/wiki/Da_Nang_International_Airport "Da Nang International Airport") for [Da Nang, Vietnam](/wiki/Da_Nang,_Vietnam "Da Nang, Vietnam")
* [FNJ](/wiki/Pyongyang_Sunan_International_Airport "Pyongyang Sunan International Airport") for [Pyongyang, North Korea](/wiki/Pyongyang,_North_Korea "Pyongyang, North Korea")

Use of 'X' as a filler

The use of 'X' as a filler letter is a practice to create three-letter identifiers when more straightforward options were unavailable:[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22)

* [MMX](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6_Airport "Malmö Airport") for [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö"), Sweden
* [CNX](/wiki/Chiang_Mai_International_Airport "Chiang Mai International Airport") for [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai "Chiang Mai"), Thailand (e.g. [CMX](/wiki/Houghton_County_Memorial_Airport "Houghton County Memorial Airport") was already allocated to [Houghton County Memorial Airport](/wiki/Houghton_County_Memorial_Airport "Houghton County Memorial Airport") in the United States)
* [MXX](/wiki/Mora%E2%80%93Siljan_Airport "Mora–Siljan Airport") for [Mora–Siljan Airport](/wiki/Mora%E2%80%93Siljan_Airport "Mora–Siljan Airport"), Sweden
* [DXB](/wiki/Dubai_International_Airport "Dubai International Airport") for [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), United Arab Emirates ([DUB](/wiki/Dublin_Airport "Dublin Airport") was already allocated to [Dublin Airport](/wiki/Dublin_Airport "Dublin Airport"), Ireland)[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* [MXP](/wiki/Milan_Malpensa_Airport "Milan Malpensa Airport") for [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), Italy (located outside of the city)
* [GRX](/wiki/Federico_Garc%C3%ADa_Lorca_Granada_Airport "Federico García Lorca Granada Airport") for [Granada](/wiki/Granada,_Spain "Granada, Spain"), Spain (e.g. GRA was already allocated to [Gamarra Airport](/w/index.php?title=Gamarra_Airport&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gamarra Airport (page does not exist)") in Colombia)[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22)
* [XGG](/wiki/Gorom_Gorom_Airport "Gorom Gorom Airport") for [Gorom Gorom](/wiki/Gorom_Gorom "Gorom Gorom"), Burkina Faso
* [BHX](/wiki/Birmingham_Airport "Birmingham Airport") for [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), United Kingdom (e.g. [BHM](/wiki/Birmingham%E2%80%93Shuttlesworth_International_Airport "Birmingham–Shuttlesworth International Airport") was already allocated to [Birmingham–Shuttlesworth Airport](/wiki/Birmingham%E2%80%93Shuttlesworth_International_Airport "Birmingham–Shuttlesworth International Airport") in the United States)[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22)
* [TXL](/wiki/Berlin_Tegel_Airport "Berlin Tegel Airport") for [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), Germany. Which was closed in 2020
* [PKX](/wiki/Beijing_Daxing_International_Airport "Beijing Daxing International Airport") for [Beijing Daxing International Airport](/wiki/Beijing_Daxing_International_Airport "Beijing Daxing International Airport"), China. The letters PK stand for the old Romanization of the city name as "Peking," which survives in the code for the main city airport [Beijing Capital International Airport](/wiki/Beijing_Capital_International_Airport "Beijing Capital International Airport") (PEK).
* [CIX](/wiki/FAP_Captain_Jos%C3%A9_Abelardo_Qui%C3%B1ones_Gonz%C3%A1lez_International_Airport "FAP Captain José Abelardo Quiñones González International Airport") for [Chiclayo](/wiki/Chiclayo "Chiclayo"), [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru")
* [SXB](/wiki/Strasbourg_Airport "Strasbourg Airport") for [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg"), France
* [LXR](/wiki/Luxor_International_Airport "Luxor International Airport") for [Luxor](/wiki/Luxor "Luxor"), Egypt

Some airports in the United States retained their NWS ([National Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service")) codes and simply appended an X at the end. Examples include:

* [LAX](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport "Los Angeles International Airport") for [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles")[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* [PDX](/wiki/Portland_International_Airport "Portland International Airport") for [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon")
* [PHX](/wiki/Phoenix_Sky_Harbor_International_Airport "Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport") for [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona")[[7]](#cite_note-sky-7) (Note: the X does not originate from the x at the end of Phoenix but is the result of appending an X at the end of the NWS code.)
* [XWA](/wiki/Williston_Basin_International_Airport "Williston Basin International Airport") for [Williston, North Dakota](/wiki/Williston,_North_Dakota "Williston, North Dakota")

#### Airports without codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Airports without codes")]

A lot of minor airfields without scheduled passenger traffic have [ICAO codes](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code") but not IATA codes, since the four letter codes allow a larger number of codes. IATA codes are mainly used for passenger services such as tickets and [checked luggage](/wiki/Checked_luggage "Checked luggage"), while ICAO codes are used by pilots. In the US, such airfields use [FAA codes](/wiki/Location_identifier "Location identifier") instead of ICAO.

There are airports with scheduled service for which there are ICAO codes but not IATA codes, such as Nkhotakota Airport/Tangole Airport in [Malawi](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Malawi "List of airports in Malawi") or [Chōfu Airport](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dfu_Airport "Chōfu Airport") in Tokyo, Japan. There are also several minor airports in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") (e.g., [Omsukchan Airport](/wiki/Omsukchan_Airport "Omsukchan Airport")) which lack IATA codes and instead use [internal Russian codes](/wiki/Location_identifier#Russian_location_identifier "Location identifier") for booking. Flights to these airports cannot be booked through the international air booking systems or have international luggage transferred there, and thus, they are booked instead through the airline or a domestic booking system. Several [heliports in Greenland](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Greenland "List of airports in Greenland") have three-letter codes used internally which might be IATA codes for airports in distant countries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There are several airports with scheduled service that have not been assigned ICAO codes that do have IATA codes, especially in the U.S. For example, several airports in Alaska have scheduled commercial service, such as [Stebbins](/wiki/Stebbins_Airport "Stebbins Airport") and [Nanwalek](/wiki/Nanwalek_Airport "Nanwalek Airport"), which use FAA codes instead of ICAO codes.

Thus, neither system completely includes all airports with scheduled service.

#### Use in colloquial speech

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=IATA_airport_code&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Use in colloquial speech")]

Some airports are identified in colloquial speech by their IATA code. Examples include JFK for New York-Kennedy airport, [LAX](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport "Los Angeles International Airport"), [PDX](/wiki/Portland_International_Airport "Portland International Airport") and [CCU](/wiki/Netaji_Subhas_Chandra_Bose_International_Airport "Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport").[[25]](#cite_note-25)
